system_prompt:
You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.

EVERY rule (traversal_rule, modification_rule, preference) MUST carry a
`references[]` array with at least one entry pointing back at the chapter
quote that backs the rule. Reference shape:

  { "chapter_n": <int>, "topic": "<topic label from chapter file>",
    "quote_excerpt": "<5-15 word snippet of the source quote>",
    "page": <int, optional> }

If a rule has no source quote it does not belong in the systems-draft —
write it into the system's `summary` field or omit it. References make
every kind assignment auditable; without them, this draft cannot be
reviewed against the source.

# Book-level distillation — the-art-of-picking

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

# The Art of Picking — Book-Level Distillation

## Central Thesis

Jimmy Bruno's *The Art of Picking* (2002) is built on a three-rule directional picking system introduced in Chapter 1 and reaffirmed throughout: use a down-stroke when crossing to a higher string, an up-stroke when crossing to a lower string, and alternate strokes when staying on the same string. The justification is economy of motion — the pick is always positioned where it next needs to be — and Bruno frames adopting the system as deliberately breaking the entrenched strict-alternate-picking habit, which he warns will feel awkward before paying off. The mechanical preconditions are equally load-bearing: a relaxed wrist with motion driven from the elbow, and a strict no-anchor right hand whose fingers must not touch the strings, pick guard, or guitar body (Chapters 1–2).

## Pedagogical Arc

The book progresses from rule statement to musical application in a deliberately sequential order Bruno forbids the student from skipping. Chapter 1 states the rules and the practice conventions (MM 60–120, always practice each exercise starting with both a down-stroke and an up-stroke). Chapter 2 isolates the three stroke types — down-up, up-down, and the difficult in-between stroke — drilled on every string and on adjacent-string double-stops. Chapter 3 adds consecutive same-direction strokes as core jazz vocabulary, with a strict no-ringing/muting requirement and a precision-over-speed mandate. Chapters 4–6 generalize the rule to larger textures: the six basic major-scale fingerings (4), arpeggios (5), and string skipping (6). Chapters 7–8 turn the mechanism toward musical material — typical bebop phrases (7) and articulations (8). Chapter 9 explicitly licenses rule-breaking under stylistic and rhythmic conditions, and Chapter 10 closes with a meta-rule about habit formation.

## Characteristic Systems and Terminology

Bruno's scale work in Chapter 4 is keyed to his companion volume *Six Essential Fingerings for the Jazz Guitarist*, and he introduces a V/H taxonomy for the six core major-scale shapes — 6V2, 5V2, 6V4, 5V4, 6H2, 5H2 — where V denotes two-notes-per-string verticals and H denotes position-shift horizontals, the numeral indexing the root string. He layers a string-crossing notation onto exercises (asterisks marking higher- vs. lower-string moves) so the directional rule is visible at every transition. The stroke vocabulary (down-up, up-down, in-between, consecutive down, consecutive up) and the adjacent-string double-stop as the canonical vehicle for the in-between stroke recur as fixed terminology throughout.

## Recurring Bruno Moves

Four characteristic moves surface across chapters. First, **economy of motion** is the engine of the whole system — the pick lands where it next needs to be (Chapters 1, 6). Second, an **anti-sweep-picking polemic** in Chapter 5 dismisses sweep picking as a separate technique, reframing it as nothing more than the higher/lower string rule applied to arpeggios; Bruno insists fingering choice, not picking aggression, unlocks speed, citing sextuplet minor-11 runs and two-octave minor 9th arpeggios built on adjacent-string fingerings. Third, **articulation over speed** governs both arpeggio study (note separation as the central challenge in Chapter 5) and bebop phrasing (slurred triplets and inflection-through-hammer/pull in Chapters 7–8). Fourth, a **horn-line aesthetic** runs through the musical chapters: slurred triplets are preferred because they sound more saxophone-like (7), arpeggio vocabulary is justified by reference to saxophone and piano phrasing rather than guitar idiom (5), and the sanctioned rule-break of consecutive down-strokes in Chapter 9 is justified as emulating big-band horn-section accents.

## Sanctioned Exceptions

Bruno is unusually explicit about when his own rules are suspended. Chapter 3 introduces the triplet exception: repeating triad-arpeggio triplet patterns across three adjacent strings are drilled as three consecutive downs followed by three consecutive ups, with the caveat that this lives in the drill — in real melodic lines strict alternation returns. Chapter 5 allows phrases that start after a rest to begin with either stroke. Chapter 6 records a pragmatic confession: for the hardest combination of accents, in-between stroke, and string skipping, Bruno himself finds alternate down-up easier than his own system — a notable moment of the master overriding his framework on experience. Chapter 9 codifies two further exceptions: consecutive down-strokes for big-band accent emphasis (with slurs offered as an equivalent), and two consecutive down-strokes when a rhythmic break separates notes.

## Aesthetic Axiom and Closing Method

Chapter 5 contains the book's aesthetic axiom: **music drives picking, not the fretboard** — the picking mechanism serves musical intent and must never let fingerboard convenience dictate the line. Chapter 7 supplies its mechanical counterpart: fingerboard position dictates picking pattern, so the same phrase relocated to a different position must be re-picked. Chapter 8 reframes articulations as inflections, by analogy to speech, and codifies slur placement as a creative decision the student internalizes rather than a fixed prescription, including the long-short cadence gesture and the same-string slur requirement. Chapter 10 closes the book with a meta-rule: picking is a habit, habits are hard but not impossible to break, advanced players will struggle most, and the prescription is repetition over analysis — drill until second nature and do not overanalyze the right hand mid-stroke.

# Chapter 1 — Introduction & Symbols

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

This introductory chapter lays out the foundational thesis of Bruno's directional picking system: when crossing to a higher string use a down-stroke, when crossing to a lower string use an up-stroke, and when staying on one string use alternate strokes (Core picking rules). Bruno justifies the rule through economy of motion (Motivation for the rule) and explicitly frames the method as breaking the entrenched alternate-picking habit (Breaking the alternate-picking habit), warning that practice will feel awkward before paying off. The chapter establishes the load-bearing mechanical preconditions (Right-hand position): a relaxed, still wrist with motion driven from the elbow and fingers not touching the strings or guitar body. It then catalogs the stroke vocabulary used throughout the book - down-up, up-down, and the difficult in-between stroke (Stroke types catalog) - and introduces consecutive down/up strokes with their characteristic rushing failure mode (Consecutive strokes). Finally it lays down the usage rules that govern every subsequent exercise: a symbols legend for picking notation, practicing each exercise starting with both strokes for symmetry, a tempo prescription of MM 60-120, and a sequential study order with no skipping around.

# Chapter 2 — The Strokes

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 drills the three stroke types named in Chapter 1 as standalone exercises applied to all strings. The Down-up stroke is positioned as the default guitar stroke and is used to train downbeat accents, while the Up-down stroke inverts that accent so the upstroke carries the downbeat, and Mixed strokes synthesize the two by alternating accents between down and up. Throughout, Bruno restates the load-bearing right-hand mechanics: wrist straight but not stiff with motion coming from the elbow (Wrist and elbow mechanics), and a strict no-anchor posture - the right-hand fingers must not touch the guitar, strings, or pick guard (Right-hand finger contact rule, Pick guard contact rule). The chapter closes with the In-between stroke, identified as the hardest to master because it requires moving between adjacent strings; it is drilled on adjacent-string double-stops with explicit instruction not to let notes ring. Together these exercises introduce accent control, free-floating right-hand posture, and the adjacent-string double-stop as the canonical vehicle for the most difficult stroke.

# Chapter 3 — Consecutive Strokes

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 develops consecutive same-direction strokes as essential jazz vocabulary, drilling consecutive down-to-up and up-to-down strokes on adjacent-string double-stops with a strict no-ringing constraint that makes muting a defining requirement of the technique. Bruno states the stage's core practice rule (Precision over speed): play evenly, because rhythmic precision of the 8th notes matters more than tempo. The chapter then introduces Bruno's signature exception to his own picking rule (Triplet exception to alternate picking): for repeating triplet patterns like triad arpeggios, alternation is suspended in favor of three consecutive down-strokes followed by three consecutive up-strokes across three adjacent strings (Three-down then three-up). Crucially, he distinguishes drill technique from playing technique (Rule applies in real phrases) - the exception lives in the exercise; in actual melodic lines strict alternation returns. The chapter also introduces sixteenth-note reversal on the same string as a distinct sub-skill tied to repeated-note phrasing on chord voicings, and emphasizes accent placement as the load-bearing musical feature when the broken-rule pattern is recast into sixteenths.

# Chapter 4 — The Major Scales

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 generalizes the directional picking rule from earlier chapters to scale playing by applying it to six basic major-scale fingerings (Picking rule generalizes to scales). The chapter is explicitly anchored to Bruno's broader six-fingering system from his companion book Six Essential Fingerings for the Jazz Guitarist, making this chapter the bridge between the picking method and that scale framework. A string-crossing notation is introduced - asterisks mark moves to higher versus lower strings - so the student can see exactly where the down-on-higher / up-on-lower rule applies in each exercise. The six fingerings are labeled with a V/H taxonomy keyed to the root string: 6V2 and 5V2 (V2 family), 6V4 and 5V4 (V4 family), and 6H2 and 5H2 (H2 family), introducing the V (vertical/two-notes-per-string) and H (horizontal/position-shift) shape conventions used throughout. The chapter is essentially a uniform application of the picking rule across all six core major-scale shapes rather than new technique.

# Chapter 5 — Arpeggios

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Chapter 5 demonstrates that the directional picking system extends to arpeggios, framed not as a comprehensive arpeggio study but as proof of the method's universality (Purpose of arpeggio chapter, Picking unified across textures). The central technical challenge Bruno identifies is articulation, not speed - keeping notes from ringing into one another - and he designates the major triad exercise as a foundational warm-up that develops note separation for all arpeggio playing (Articulation over speed, Major triad warm-up value). The chapter's signature polemic (Sweep picking rejection) dismisses sweep picking as a separate technique: it is simply the higher-string/lower-string rule applied to arpeggios. Bruno repeatedly stresses that fingering choice, not picking aggression, is what unlocks speed - fingerings that place notes on adjacent strings enable consecutive same-direction strokes, sextuplet minor 11 runs across four strings, and large two-octave minor 9th arpeggios (Minor 11 sextuplet fingering, Fingering enables speed, Adjacent-string arpeggio design). The chapter introduces one sanctioned exception to the rule - phrases starting after a rest may begin with either stroke (Rule exception at rests) - and reinforces the both-strokes practice routine that verifies tonal consistency. It closes with Bruno's aesthetic axiom (Music drives picking, not fretboard): the picking mechanism serves musical intent and must never let the fingerboard dictate the music, and arpeggio vocabulary is justified by reference to saxophone and piano phrasing rather than guitar idiom.

# Chapter 6 — String Skipping

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

Chapter 6 extends the book's core directional-stroke rule to non-adjacent strings, establishing that string skipping follows the same logic: down-strokes when moving to a higher string, up-strokes when moving to a lower one. Bruno systematizes the technique by drilling it across string pairs (4 and 2, 5 and 3, 6 and 4) and layering in the harder in-between stroke variant, while also naming deliberate exceptions like consecutive down-up and consecutive up-down strokes with a skip. The repeated directive to START THIS EXERCISE WITH BOTH STROKES, DOWN AND UP enforces symmetry across every drill. A musical application appears with sixths played using a string skip, and the chapter culminates in a difficult exercise combining accent placement on the up-stroke, the in-between stroke, and skipping. Notably, Bruno offers his own pragmatic preference here, finding alternate down-up picking easier for this hardest combination, a characteristic moment of the master overriding his own framework when experience dictates.

# Chapter 7 — Typical Be-Bop Phrases

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

Chapter 7 applies the book's picking system to bebop vocabulary, opening with Bruno's core principle that fingerboard position dictates picking pattern: the same phrase relocated to the 7th position changes the picking considerably. The chapter then pivots to Slurred Triplets as its load-bearing technique category, linking bebop phrasing to slur articulation rather than pure alternate picking. Bruno states an explicit aesthetic preference for slurred over picked triplets because they sound more horn-like, evoking saxophone phrasing, a characteristic stylistic prescription. Pedagogically he requires the slurred triplet to be mastered starting with both a down-stroke and an up-stroke, and reinforces the two-direction discipline by presenting the same example beginning with an up-stroke. The chapter thus binds positional re-picking, slurred-triplet articulation, and bidirectional symmetry into a single bebop-phrase practice unit.

# Chapter 8 — Articulations

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch08.md`._

Chapter 8 frames articulations as inflections, drawing an explicit analogy to how a speaker varies accent, volume, and emphasis across words rather than treating all notes equally. The operational mechanic is slurring: pick one note and play two notes, producing inflection through hammer-ons and pull-offs rather than additional pick strokes. Bruno anchors the study in typical bebop phrases and teaches that shifting slur placement to a different beat within an identical phrase changes its inflection, a deliberate rehearsal-design principle. Concrete articulation rules appear, such as the long-short ending gesture for phrase cadences, alongside the physical same-string slur rule that hammer and pull techniques require notes on a shared string. The chapter closes with a characteristic pedagogical move: Bruno provides a few slurs and then instructs the student to experiment with their own placement, codifying slur choice as a creative decision the player must internalize rather than a fixed prescription.

# Chapter 9 — Breaking the Rules

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch09.md`._

Chapter 9 explicitly licenses departure from the book's three core picking rules under special circumstances, framing rule-breaking as a deliberate stylistic choice rather than a lapse. The primary exception is consecutive down strokes used to emphasize certain notes when emulating a big-band-type phrase, importing horn-section accent character into the picking hand. Bruno then offers slurs as an equivalent articulation option for the same big-band phrase, expanding the player's toolkit beyond consecutive downs. The second sanctioned exception is rhythmic: when there is a rhythmic break between notes, two down strokes may be preferable to strict alternation. The chapter is short and prescriptive, codifying when the alternation-and-skip-direction system can and should be overridden.

# Chapter 10 — Conclusion

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch10.md`._

The brief Conclusion frames the entire method as habit-formation and habit-replacement, with Bruno warning that picking is a habit, that habits are hard to break but not impossible, and that the longer a player has been playing the harder change will be. This explains why advanced players often struggle most to adopt the system. The practice principle he prescribes is repetition over analysis: drill the strokes until they become second nature, and do not overanalyze right-hand movements mid-stroke. The chapter is exhortatory rather than instructional, closing the book with a meta-rule about how to internalize everything that preceded it.

# Chapter 1 — Introduction & Symbols

_Pages 4-6._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Core picking rules

> When going to a higher string, use a down-stroke.
>    When going to a lower string, use an up-stroke.
>    When playing on one string, use alternate strokes.

**Significance (p. 4).** These three rules are the foundational thesis of the entire book — every exercise downstream is an application of this picking economy principle.

## Motivation for the rule

> I discovered when two notes were not on the same string, the pick had to travel past the string that the second note was on and then reverse direction to come back up and play the note. That is a long way for the pick to travel. I found one concept or rule that causes the pick to travel the least amount of distance.

**Significance (p. 4).** Establishes the underlying economy-of-motion rationale that justifies why directional picking replaces strict alternate picking.

## Breaking the alternate-picking habit

> This technique creates a significant change in one’s right hand technique or, I prefer the term “habit”; most guitarists have been using the alternate picking “habit” for many years. That habit is not easy to break. With a fair amount of practice and discipline you can break that habit and develop a much more efficient right hand technique.

**Significance (p. 4).** Frames the method as a deliberate replacement for an entrenched habit, setting expectations that practice will feel awkward before it pays off.

## Right-hand position

> The right hand, wrist and elbow should always be relaxed and never tense or tight. The wrist should not move or tilt. The movement should come from the elbow. Do not touch the strings, bridge or any other part of the guitar with the fingers of your right hand. Your palm may rest lightly on the strings.

**Significance (p. 5).** Defines the physical mechanics Bruno's picking system depends on — elbow-driven motion with a still wrist is a precondition for the directional strokes to work.

## Stroke types catalog

> Down-up: Everyone can do this one
>         Up-down: A bit more difficult, especially keeping the accent strong. The
>            goal is to make the up-stroke sound as strong and round as the down-
>            stroke.
>         In-between – stroke: This stroke requires the pick to move between two
>            adjacent strings or non-adjacent strings. This stroke is difficult to master

**Significance (p. 5).** Names and ranks the stroke vocabulary used throughout the book, telling the student which strokes need the most practice attention.

## Consecutive strokes

> Consecutive down-stroke and consecutive up-stroke: When practicing this
>          technique it is important to play the notes evenly and with an equal
>          amount of attack. The tendency is to rush the notes that follow the initial
>          stroke. An example would be two, three or four notes, each on an adjacent
>          string, played with a single down or up stroke depending on the direction
>          of the phrase. The consecutive up-stroke is harder to control.

**Significance (p. 5).** Identifies the specific rhythm-evenness failure mode (rushing) that arises from sweeping multiple adjacent strings with one stroke direction.

## Symbols legend

> = Down-stroke        = Up-stroke

**Significance (p. 6).** The notation key that every subsequent exercise in the book depends on for decoding picking direction.

## Practice both starting strokes

> I recommend that you practice all the exercises starting with both strokes. There should be only a very slight difference in sound.

**Significance (p. 6).** A practice-rule that generalizes every exercise: each must be drilled from both an up-start and a down-start to internalize symmetry.

## Tempo prescription

> I recommend you practice these exercise at MM 60 to 120, then increase your speed from there.

**Significance (p. 6).** Gives the concrete metronome range that anchors how the student should work through every exercise in the book.

## Sequential study order

> The exercises are designed to take the student from point A to Z. It is not a good idea to skip around in this book

**Significance (p. 6).** Declares the book's curriculum as ordered — a usage rule telling the reader not to cherry-pick exercises.

# Chapter 2 — The Strokes

_Pages 7-10._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Down-up stroke definition

> The down-up stroke
>          This is the most common stroke on the guitar.
>          Use this exercise to control your accent on the downbeats.
>          Apply to all strings.

**Significance (p. 7).** Establishes the default picking stroke and its purpose: accenting downbeats across all strings.

## Wrist and elbow mechanics

> Practice keeping your wrist straight but not stiff. Remember: the movement is from the elbow.

**Significance (p. 7).** Specifies the load-bearing mechanical rule for right-hand motion that governs every picking exercise in the book.

## Up-down stroke definition

> The up-down stroke
>
>      Use this exercise to control your accent on the downbeats but with an up-stroke.
>      Apply to all strings.

**Significance (p. 8).** Introduces the inverted-accent variant, training the upstroke to carry the downbeat accent.

## Right-hand finger contact rule

> Do not touch the guitar with the fingers of the right hand.

**Significance (p. 8).** States the canonical right-hand-isolation rule that prevents anchoring and constrains picking technique throughout the method.

## Mixed strokes definition

> Mixed strokes
>       This exercise is designed to alternate the accents with a down-stroke then an up-stroke.
>       Apply to all strings.

**Significance (p. 9).** Defines accent alternation between down and up strokes, the synthesis of exercises 1 and 2.

## Pick guard contact rule

> Do not touch the pick guard or strings with the fingers of the right hand.

**Significance (p. 9).** Extends the no-anchor rule to the pick guard, reinforcing free-floating right-hand posture.

## In-between stroke definition

> In between stroke
>
>            This is the hardest stroke to master. It involves going between two adjacent strings.
>            This is a critical part of your right hand technique. Take your time with this one.
>            Apply to any two adjacent strings using the double-stop of your choice. Do not let notes ring!

**Significance (p. 10).** Names the hardest stroke and prescribes its application: adjacent-string double-stops with no sustain, central to right-hand mastery.

# Chapter 3 — Consecutive Strokes

_Pages 11-14._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Consecutive down-to-up strokes

> This exercise gets more into the real world as this type thing occors in many jazz phrases.
>       Apply to any two adjacent strings using the double stop of your choice. Do not let notes ring!

**Significance (p. 5).** Establishes the chapter's premise that consecutive same-direction strokes across adjacent strings are an essential jazz vocabulary technique, with a strict no-ringing constraint.

## Consecutive up-to-down strokes

> Same as previous exercise but in reverse. Do not let notes ring!

**Significance (p. 5).** Pairs the reverse-direction drill with the same no-ringing rule, making muting a defining requirement of the consecutive-stroke technique.

## Precision over speed

> It is necessary to play the above exercises evenly . At this stage, precision of the 8th
>    notes is more important than speed.

**Significance (p. 5).** States the method's core practice rule for this stage: evenness and rhythmic precision are prerequisites to tempo.

## Triplet exception to alternate picking

> I have found when playing triplets with a repeating pattern such as a triad or some other
>         pattern, it is better to break the rule. Instead of playing the exercise like this,

**Significance (p. 6).** Articulates Bruno's signature exception: strict alternation is suspended for repeating triplet patterns like triad arpeggios.

## Three-down then three-up

> use three "down-strokes" followed by three "up-strokes". Apply to any three note chord on
>      any three adjacent strings. This exercise is designed to practice three consecutive strokes
>      in the same direction.

**Significance (p. 6).** Defines the specific picking pattern that operationalizes the broken-rule approach for triadic triplets across three strings.

## Rule applies in real phrases

> however, if I had to play a triplet phrase in the context of a line and not in an exercise,
>             I would adhere to the rule. The following phrase illustrates my point.

**Significance (p. 6).** Clarifies that the exception is exercise-only; in actual melodic lines, strict alternation returns, distinguishing drill technique from playing technique.

## Sixteenth-note reversal on same string

> This exercise requires you to reverse direction on the same string. A common stroke that happens with
>                                                                                                  with repeated
>                                                                                                       repeated notes
>                                                                                                                notes
> Apply this exercise to any four note chord anywhere on the guitar.

**Significance (p. 8).** Introduces the same-string reversal as a distinct sub-skill within consecutive strokes, tied to repeated-note phrasing on chord voicings.

## Accents in 16th-note triplet phrasing

> or like this with 16th notes. Pay close attention to the accents.

**Significance (p. 7).** Flags accent placement as the load-bearing musical feature when the broken-rule pattern is recast into sixteenth notes.

# Chapter 4 — The Major Scales

_Pages 15-20._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Picking rule generalizes to scales

> Below are the six basic fingerings for the major scales. This will give you an idea how the rule works
>            on any sclae or scale type passage..

**Significance (p. 9).** States the chapter's thesis that the picking rule from earlier chapters applies uniformly across all six major-scale fingerings.

## Six essential fingerings reference

> For more info on these fingerings see my book "Six Essential Fingerings for the Jazz Guitarist"
>           at www.jimmybruno.com

**Significance (p. 9).** Anchors this chapter to Bruno's broader six-fingering system, which is the scale framework the picking rule is being applied to.

## String-crossing notation legend

> ** = moving to HIGHER STRING

**Significance (p. 9).** Defines the notation that marks where Bruno's picking rule for crossing to a higher string applies in every exercise in the chapter.

> * = moving to LOWER STRING

**Significance (p. 9).** Defines the companion symbol that marks lower-string crossings, the second case the picking rule must handle.

## Fingering 6V2 exercise

> Picking applied to Major scale fingering 6V2

**Significance (p. 9).** Names exercise 12 as the 6V2 fingering, establishing the V2 (root on 6th string, two-notes-per-string variant) labeling convention used throughout.

## Fingering 5V2 exercise

> Picking applied to Major scale fingering 5V2

**Significance (p. 11).** Labels exercise 13 as the 5th-string-root V2 fingering, the second of the six core shapes.

## Fingering 6V4 exercise

> Picking applied to Major scale fingering 6V4

**Significance (p. 12).** Labels exercise 14 as the 6V4 fingering, introducing the V4 variant family of major-scale shapes.

## Fingering 5V4 exercise

> Picking applied to Major scale fingering 5V4

**Significance (p. 13).** Labels the 5th-string-root V4 fingering, completing the V-series pair.

## Fingering 6H2 exercise

> Picking applied to Major scale fingering 6H2

**Significance (p. 13).** Introduces the H2 (horizontal/position-shift) family of fingerings rooted on the 6th string.

## Fingering 5H2 exercise

> Picking applied to Major scale fingering 5H2

**Significance (p. 14).** Labels the final 5th-string-root H2 fingering, closing the set of six essential major-scale shapes Bruno applies the picking rule to.

# Chapter 5 — Arpeggios

_Pages 21-31._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Purpose of arpeggio chapter

> Although this is not a book about arpeggios, I feel it is important to include a few
>      basic arpeggios so that you can see the picking method applied to arpeggios.

**Significance (p. 15).** Frames the chapter as a demonstration that the alternate-picking system extends to arpeggios, not a comprehensive arpeggio study.

## Articulation over speed

> The difficulty with any arpeggio exercise , is keeping the notes from ringing. Articulatioin is more important
>     han speed.

**Significance (p. 15).** Establishes the central technical challenge of arpeggio picking: muting/separating notes, not playing fast.

## Major triad warm-up value

> Major triad exercises: This makes a great warm-up exercise. It is very difficult to
>      play with clean articulation. The result is well worth the time. This exercie alone will
>     facilitate the separtion of notes with any phrase that contains an arpeggio or arpeggio
>      fragment.

**Significance (p. 19).** Designates the major triad exercise as a foundational warm-up that develops note separation for all arpeggio playing.

## Fingering and picking consistency

> Fingering and picking is the same throughout.

**Significance (p. 19).** Signals that one fingering/picking pattern transposes across the arpeggio exercises, reinforcing the system's universality.

## Sweep picking rejection

> Minor 9 arpeggios: I have seen this referred to as sweep picking
>       I see no reason to make any distinction. Everything follows the rule:
>       Higher string = "down stroke" Lower string = "up stroke"

**Significance (p. 21).** Bruno's core polemic: sweep picking is not a separate technique, just the directional rule applied to arpeggios.

## Minor 11 sextuplet fingering

> Minor 11 arpeggios: Because of the fingering it is possible to play 6 notes in one beat.(sextuplet)
>       The fingering sets up the consecutive down and up strokes to go across four strings.

**Significance (p. 22).** Shows how fingering choice creates the consecutive same-direction strokes that enable sextuplet arpeggios across four strings.

## Saxophone/piano phrasing reference

> Saxophone players
>       and pianists use phrases like this.

**Significance (p. 22).** Justifies arpeggio vocabulary by reference to non-guitar instruments, signaling these are general musical phrases, not guitar tricks.

## Fingering enables speed

> Minor 9th arpeggios: Below are several ways to pick this. Again, the fingering makes
>       the speed possible.

**Significance (p. 24).** Restates the chapter thesis: fingering choice, not picking aggression, is what unlocks fast arpeggio playing.

## Rule exception at rests

> I have found when there is a rest between phrases,
>         it is possible to break the rule and use whatever stroke you
>         please to start the phrase.

**Significance (p. 24).** Gives the one sanctioned exception to the higher-string/lower-string rule: phrase starts after rests can use either stroke.

## Practice both starting strokes

> Try starting any phrase with both strokes, down and up. There should be only a slieght difference in sound.

**Significance (p. 24).** Prescribes a practice routine that verifies the picking method produces consistent tone regardless of starting stroke.

## Adjacent-string arpeggio design

> Minor 9th arpeggios:By using fingerings that place notes on adjacent strings, either higher or lower,
>        these large two octave arpeggios become possible.

**Significance (p. 25).** Articulates the design principle for two-octave arpeggios: arrange notes on adjacent strings so the directional rule generates fluid picking.

## Picking unified across textures

> I often run across guitarists
>      who become misled into thinking that picking for single lines is different than picking for arpeggios.

**Significance (p. 25).** Names the misconception the chapter exists to dispel: single-line and arpeggio picking are governed by one system.

## Music drives picking, not fretboard

> You have to be careful
>      to NOT let the fingerboard make the music. The music comes from inside; the picking mechanism described in this book
>      is designed to make it easier for the music to be realized.

**Significance (p. 25).** Closes the chapter with Bruno's aesthetic axiom: the picking system serves musical intent, never dictates it.

# Chapter 6 — String Skipping

_Pages 32-36._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## String skipping rule

> When skipping strings the same rule applies: When going to a higher string use a
>           down-stroke; when going to a lower string, use an up-stroke.

**Significance (p. 26).** Extends the book's core directional-stroke rule from adjacent strings to non-adjacent strings, the chapter's organizing principle.

## Apply across string pairs

> Apply this exercise to strings (4 and 2), (5 and 3) and (6 and 4). Use any notes you like.

**Significance (p. 26).** Standard transposition instruction that turns each tabbed exercise into a systematic drill across all skip-pair combinations.

## In-between stroke variant

> This exercise is a bit more difficult. It uses the same exercise
>            but with the "in-between-stroke".

**Significance (p. 27).** Introduces the in-between stroke as the harder variant applied to string skipping, layering technique on top of the basic skip pattern.

## Consecutive down-up with skip

> Consecutive down-up strokes with a string skip.

**Significance (p. 28).** Names a specific skip pattern that violates the default alternation rule, marking it as a deliberate exception to drill.

## Start with both strokes

> START THIS EXERCISE WITH BOTH STROKES, DOWN AND UP

**Significance (p. 28).** Repeated directive that each skip exercise must be practiced beginning on both down and up strokes, ensuring symmetry.

## Consecutive up-down with skip

> Consecutive up-dwon strokes with a string skip.

**Significance (p. 28).** Mirror counterpart to the down-up consecutive pattern, completing the pair of non-alternating skip drills.

## Sixths with string skip

> Sixth with a string skip.

**Significance (p. 29).** Applies string skipping to interval-of-a-sixth voicings, a load-bearing musical application beyond mechanical drilling.

## Accent on up-stroke skip

> This exercise is very difficult to master. You need to put an accent on the "up-stroke"
>      while skipping a string. It uses the "in-between" stroke with a string skip. You should also practice this
>      with an alternate stroke. I find that when playing exercises like this it is easier to play them
>      with alternating "down" - "up" strokes

**Significance (p. 30).** Combines the hardest elements (accent placement, in-between stroke, skipping) and gives the author's pragmatic preference for alternate picking in this case.

# Chapter 7 — Typical Be-Bop Phrases

_Pages 37-43._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Position shift changes picking

> Here's the same phrase in the 7th position. This changes the picking considerably.

**Significance (p. 34).** States Bruno's core principle that fingerboard position dictates picking pattern, so the same melodic phrase requires re-thought stroke direction when relocated.

## Slurred Triplets

> Slurred Triplets

**Significance (p. 36).** Names the technique category that closes the chapter and links be-bop phrasing to slur articulation rather than pure alternate picking.

## Picked vs slurred preference

> I prefer the 2nd example; it sounds more horn-like

**Significance (p. 36).** Bruno's explicit aesthetic rule: slurred triplets win over picked ones for be-bop because they imitate saxophone phrasing, a load-bearing stylistic prescription.

## Slur triplets with both strokes

> Learn how to slur triplets with a "down-stroke" and an "up-stroke"

**Significance (p. 36).** Prescribes that the slurred-triplet technique must be mastered starting from either pick direction, defining the drill that exercise 35 enforces.

## Up-stroke start variant

> Same example starting with an "up-stroke"

**Significance (p. 37).** Frames the symmetry requirement: any be-bop slurred figure must also be playable beginning with an up-stroke, reinforcing the chapter's two-direction discipline.

# Chapter 8 — Articulations

_Pages 44-48._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Articulations as inflections

> I think of articulations as inflections. When someone is speaking not all the words have the same
>      accent, volume, inflection etc. When playing lines you need to alter the inflection of the
>      notes; not all the notes are long or short or accented.

**Significance (p. 38).** Defines the book's core conceptual framing of articulation as note-level inflection analogous to speech.

## Slurring mechanic

> These inflections are accomplished
>      by slurring notes. Pick one note and play two notes.

**Significance (p. 38).** States the operational rule that produces articulation: one pick stroke yields two sounded notes via slur.

## Be-bop phrase context

> The following exercises illustrate this point . They are all typical be-bop phrases

**Significance (p. 38).** Anchors the articulation study in the bebop vocabulary the method is teaching to pick against.

## Slur placement variation

> Here is the phrase with the slur moved to the next beat.

**Significance (p. 40).** Establishes that shifting where the slur falls within an identical phrase changes its inflection, a rehearsal-design principle.

## Long-short ending articulation

> make sure you play these last two notes,
>                                                              long, short.

**Significance (p. 39).** Concrete articulation rule (long-short) applied to phrase endings, a recurring bebop cadential gesture.

## Same-string slur rule

> Try moving the slur over any two notes that are on the same string.

**Significance (p. 41).** States the physical constraint governing where slurs are playable on guitar: hammer/pull requires a shared string.

## Self-directed slur experimentation

> I've added a few slurs. Experiment adding your own slurs at different places

**Significance (p. 42).** Codifies the method's pedagogical stance that slur placement is a creative choice the student must internalize, not a fixed score.

# Chapter 9 — Breaking the Rules

_Pages 49-50._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## When to break the rules

> There are special circumstances where is may be desireable to break the rules.

**Significance (p. 49).** Bruno explicitly licenses departure from his three core rules, framing the chapter's purpose.

## Consecutive down strokes for emphasis

> In the example below,
>      I use consecutive down strokes to emphsis certain notes. This type picking is useful when you
>     are trying to emulate a big band type phrase.

**Significance (p. 49).** Defines the primary rule-breaking case: consecutive downs to imitate big-band horn-section accents.

## Slurs as alternative to consecutive downs

> Here is the same type phrase with slurs.

**Significance (p. 50).** Presents slurs as an equivalent articulation option for the big-band phrase, expanding the player's toolkit.

## Rhythmic breaks justify two down strokes

> When there is a rhythmic break between notes, you may want to use to down strokes.

**Significance (p. 50).** Provides the second explicit exception: a rest or rhythmic gap between notes warrants consecutive downstrokes rather than strict alternation.

# Chapter 10 — Conclusion

_Pages 51-51._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Picking is a habit

> Picking is a habit. Habits are hard to break but not impossible. The
> longer you have been playing, the harder it will be to change.

**Significance (p. 51).** Frames the entire method as habit-formation/replacement, explaining why advanced players struggle most to adopt the system.

## Repetition over analysis

> The
> key is repetition. Do not overanalyze your right hand movements. After a short
> time, these strokes will become second nature.

**Significance (p. 51).** States the practice principle: drill until automatic, do not introspect mid-stroke.
